facts_disclosed: Lease renewal June 30, rent increase 1,100 to 1,450

turns.content: got a letter from my landlord today that made me want to throw my laptop out the window. which would be ironic since i need it to earn the money he's asking for
That doesn't sound great. What's the letter about?

lease renewal. my contract is up june 30 and he wants to raise rent from 1,100 to 1,450. that's a 32% increase. for the same apartment. which has not improved in any way. the bathroom faucet still drips

A 350 euro monthly increase is brutal. Is this even legal under Berlin's rent regulations? Germany has some of the stronger tenant protections in Europe — a 32% jump might exceed what's allowed under the Mietpreisbremse or the local rent index (Mietspiegel).

Do you know what the comparable rents are in the Mietspiegel for your area and apartment type?

yeah that's the annoying part. i checked and kreuzberg mietspiegel for my building age and size actually supports something around 13-14 euro per sqm now. i have about 55 sqm so 1,450 comes out to ~26 euro/sqm which... wait actually that's way over. hold on let me recalculate. okay 1,450 / 55 is like 26.36 per sqm. and mietspiegel says 13-14. so he can't actually charge that right?

You're right to do that math. If the Mietspiegel for your area, building age, and apartment features is 13-14 euros per sqm, then 26.36 per sqm is way above what he can legally charge on a lease renewal.

Under German rent law, for an existing tenancy he can generally only raise rent up to the Mietspiegel level, and even then there's a cap — typically no more than 20% increase within 3 years (the Kappungsgrenze, which Berlin uses). Since you're paying 1,100 now, the maximum increase might be around 1,320 even if the Mietspiegel supports it.

But I'd strongly recommend checking with the Mieterverein (tenants' association) or a housing lawyer. Are you a member of a Mieterverein?

i am not because past-alex thought "i'll never need that" which is exactly the kind of short-sighted decision past-alex is known for. i'll look into joining. but even if he can only raise it to like 1,300 that's still 200 more a month for a dripping faucet and neighbors who think 1am is an appropriate time to practice drums

Past-Alex and future planning, a complicated relationship. Join the Mieterverein ASAP — most have a 3-month waiting period before they'll take your case, so the sooner you join the better. The Berliner Mieterverein is the biggest one.

As for the practical decision: even at a legal increase to ~1,300, you're right that it's more money for the same place. But the alternative is finding a new apartment in Berlin, which is its own circle of hell right now. The vacancy rate is something like 1%. Have you looked at what's actually available?

i spent 20 minutes on immoscout yesterday and almost had a panic attack. everything in kreuzberg is 1,500+ for comparable places. neukölln has some stuff around 1,200 but then i'd be further from the office, further from marius, and honestly i just like my neighborhood. the späti guy knows my name. that has value

The Späti guy knowing your name is genuinely underrated social infrastructure. Neighborhood roots matter.

So the realistic options seem to be:

1. **Fight the increase** — join Mieterverein, push back on the 1,450, negotiate down to whatever the legal maximum is (~1,300ish)
2. **Pay whatever the legal increase is and stay** — eat the extra cost, keep your neighborhood, your commute, your Späti
3. **Move somewhere cheaper** — Neukölln, Wedding, further out. Save money, lose everything else
4. **Don't renew and find something comparable** — essentially option 3 but in Kreuzberg, which at 1,500+ is even more expensive than staying

It sounds like option 1 followed by reluctant acceptance of option 2 is where you're leaning?

yeah basically. fight it down to something legal and then pay it while grumbling. the berlin housing experience. i remember when this apartment was 850 and i thought that was expensive. that was 2021. vier years ago. Quatsch

850 to 1,300+ in four years is a rough trajectory, even for Berlin. The city's housing market has genuinely gone off a cliff since the Mietendeckel got struck down.

Practical next steps:
1. Join the Berliner Mieterverein this week
2. Don't sign anything your landlord sends until you've had it reviewed
3. Respond to the letter in writing, acknowledging you received it but not agreeing to the terms
4. Get the exact Mietspiegel data for your address — the online calculator on the Berlin Senate website is actually decent

And fix the dripping faucet claim — document it. If he wants to charge more, he should be maintaining the place.

good point about the faucet. i've been too lazy to report it but if he's going to raise rent i suddenly care about maintenance a lot more. i should also probably photograph everything in the apartment for documentation. the scuff on the hallway wall. the window that doesn't seal properly. all of it

Exactly — if he wants to charge Mietspiegel rates, the apartment should meet Mietspiegel standards. Documenting everything now gives you leverage:

- Take timestamped photos of every defect
- Send a written maintenance request (email is fine, but keep records) listing all the issues
- If he doesn't fix them, that's ammunition in a rent negotiation: "the apartment has unresolved maintenance issues, a rent increase to Mietspiegel rates isn't justified when the condition is below standard"

It won't necessarily block the increase but it strengthens your position and might result in repairs you should have gotten anyway.
alright i'm going to spend tonight photographing my apartment like a crime scene. kernel will enjoy the attention. she's very photogenic when she's sitting on defective furniture

Kernel as evidence exhibit A. Include her in every photo for scale and emotional impact. Good luck with the documentation — and seriously, join the Mieterverein before you do anything else. They deal with exactly this kind of thing every day.
